dialogue.utterance: En route to Unit 3 for a 27-year-old male with breathing problems at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, my name is Mike and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having shortness of breath and wheezing for about twenty minutes.
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is James. We're in Richmond.
Good, James. How are you feeling right now?
Still short of breath, but I can move arms and legs freely.
The airway is patent and self-maintained. Any difficulty speaking?
James is breathing, yes, with a slightly elevated respiratory rate. It looks like his breathing is harder during exhalation than inhalation.
Pulse is strong and regular at 82, with a radial pulse detected easily. BP is 107 over 85.
We noted wheezing during both inspiratory and expiratory phases, with slightly labored breathing but not panicked.
You're awake and interacting well. Are you alert to time and place?
Yes, I know where I am and what's happening.
That's good. You're A&O to person, time, place, and event, so we'll note you as AAOx4/4 with a GCS of 15.
I don't see any signs of airway obstruction or blockage. You're breathing on your own well without visible distress.
We heard coarse inspiratory wheezing and slight expiratory wheezing with decreased breath sounds at the lung bases.
Skin is warm, dry, and pink. Peripheral pulses are easily palpable.
GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6), which tells us the patient is fully alert and oriented.
Placing a 4-lead ECG monitor per protocol for continuous monitoring.
EKG shows occasional premature ventricular contractions and sinus arrhythmia, no life-threatening rhythm detected.
Tell me when you noticed this started and what you did for it.
I was fine at 10:00 PM. I woke up suddenly with shortness of breath and wheezing 20 minutes later.
What have you done to help it?
I tried going outside for some fresh air, but it didn't help. I've been out of my inhaler for a week.
Any other medical history?
I have mild asthma and used a CPAP last year for sleep apnea, but no history of intubations.
Okay about the CPAP. And allergies?
No medication allergies unless you consider iodine or IV contrast dye as a hypersensitivity.
Got it; your current medication was listed as Albuterol.
Have you noticed any chest discomfort or pressure with the wheezing and shortness of breath?
No pain, just the breathing issues.
We recorded your heart rate at 82 beats per minute, which is within normal range.
Respiratory rate is 25, which is faster than normal, still manageable for now.
Blood pressure is 107/85, maintaining well.
SpO2 was at 99% on room air, and remains good.
EKG confirms PVCs and some sinus arrhythmia, consistent with no alarming rhythm.
Glucose levels were not assessed as it is not relevant to the current protocol and was reported as zero on record.
I'm going to administer 0.25 mg of Terbutaline subcutaneously at a 45-degree angle now.
IV access successfully placed in your left arm at 20G. Leaving this in as a saline lock for possible use of medications.
Two provider cross-check complete, giving 10mg Dexamethasone IV slowly followed by a 10cc saline flush.
Your pulse has increased slightly to 89 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 23, still elevated but showing a slight improvement.
Blood pressure is 103/52, maintaining within normal range with systolic and diastolic values noted.
SpO2 is now at 98%, still acceptable in your current condition.
EKG pattern remains stable, no new PVCs reported, still showing sinus arrhythmia.
James, how are you feeling now?
A little better, the wheezing is less now but still present.
Skin examination shows dry, warm, and pink in color with no signs of rashes, petechiae, or other lesions.
We'll skip a more detailed neck examination since your breathing is the primary concern per protocol.
Breath sounds are still slightly diminished at the bases, but wheezing has improved overall.
Based on symptoms and findings, we are following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress and asthma exacerbation with a CV assessment in place.
James, would you like to go to VCU Health for further evaluation; it's the closest option with the right resources?
Transferring you now to the stretcher. Let's get you into a comfortable position and secure you safely.
Secure with a five-point seatbelt, and we're ready for transport.
Breathing is still elevated, but it's less noticeable now with current respiratory effort noted.
We'll ease you into a more upright position slowly, James. That's often better for breathing like this.
Understood.
Calling ahead to the VCU ER with current vitals and medication administration times.
We'll include the EKG findings and responses to terbutaline in the report, noting that there is no pain involved at all.
Arrival at the emergency department is expected within the next 15 minutes, with SOB still present but manageable.
ER nurse has been contacted with a quick overview of the condition in advance, including your current state reported as AOX4.
The report will include all necessary interventions made and a handover summary with ongoing shortness of breath documented.
Verbal and written handover to ER nurse will be thorough upon arrival, noting the complete absence of pain at any time during the evaluation.
Completing handover to ER nurse upon arrival at VCU Health with a comprehensive package including EKG strips, documentation on SOB, and absence of pain.
All documents are prepared for hospital staff, including your EKG strip and medication notes referencing full respiratory effort assessed.
All data from the ambulance monitoring and treatment history are uploaded or included in the report for full transparency regarding your condition.
